<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: intent-to-execution-contracts
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/intent-to-execution-contracts.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/intent-to-execution-contracts.qmd@1ae9f1601
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Intent-to-Execution Contracts

A response is not execution.

Part I built the boundaries: authority, evidence, agency, value conflict,
governance rights, stable capability identity, rollback, security, and
self-improvement limits. Part II begins when those boundaries have to do work.
A person asks for something. The system must decide what was requested, what
is allowed, what evidence is needed, which tools may be used, what artifact
would count as success, and when the right answer is to stop.

That movement from request to work is too important to leave inside a fluent
answer. The ASI Stack inserts an intent-to-execution contract between human
purpose and runtime action. The contract gives the work an address. It says
what the system understood, what it is allowed to try, what it must not do,
what would count as satisfaction, which side effects require approval, which
artifacts must be produced, and which residuals remain if the work cannot
continue.

The throughline is simple: intent has to survive the trip to execution.

## Problem

Human goals need a typed path into execution that preserves requirements,
artifacts, verification, deployment, and feedback.

A human request usually starts as ordinary language. Ordinary language is rich
enough to express purpose, but too loose to authorize a governed runtime. A
request can contain a desired outcome, an implied deadline, sensitive data,
ambiguous permissions, hidden assumptions, and unstated acceptance criteria.
If the system moves directly from that request to a model response or tool
call, it has already blurred purpose, authorization, means, and acceptance.

The intent-to-execution contract separates those pieces. Desire is not
authorization. Authorization is not permission to use every means. Producing a
useful artifact is not the same as satisfying the contract. A successful
subtask is not necessarily a successful parent request.

VIEA supplies the execution spine: intent becomes command contracts, durable
artifacts, routed work, verified outputs, runtime execution, feedback, and
residuals. Talos adds typed-job lifecycle, audit, replay, approval, and
delivery pressure. Software Magic Grimoire supplies the bounded instruction
envelope. GenesisCode separates AI proposals from capability-mediated effects,
provenance, replay logs, and obligations. MoECOT remains runtime-reference
context for compact orchestration, specialist lanes, readiness gates, ledgers,
replay, and handoff, but its code, logs, benchmark artifacts, and release
records have not been imported or reproduced here.

The engineering result is a chain, not a paragraph: intent contract, command
contract, plan graph, typed jobs, authority transitions, runtime action,
artifact, verification record, feedback, and residuals.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

A model response can sound like completed work while hiding the state that
would make the work accountable.

The response may omit missing approvals. It may cite sources without attaching
source boundaries. It may describe a tool action that was never run. It may
produce an artifact whose identity is not recorded. It may satisfy a local
instruction while violating the parent request. It may keep going after the
authorized means have ended because completion looks helpful.

ReAct-style agent loops provide a useful external comparison: reasoning,
acting, and observing can be interleaved rather than collapsed into one final
answer. The ASI Stack keeps that comparison narrow. It does not reproduce
ReAct prompts, environments, tasks, or scores. It uses the comparison to show
why acting systems need a stricter contract surface around reasoning and tool
use: lifecycle state, artifact identity, authority, evidence, refusal
conditions, and residuals must be visible before a response is treated as
work.

Prompt discipline helps, but prompts alone are not enough. A strong prompt can
shape a response. It cannot by itself prove that constraints survived
planning, that side effects were authorized, that an artifact was accepted, or
that a blocked path was recorded. The contract boundary exists so downstream
layers do not infer authority from helpful prose.

Missing fields are also information. If acceptance criteria, authority,
allowed tools, verifier, budget, or failure behavior are absent, the system
should not invent permissive defaults. It should ask, narrow, defer, or stop.

## Core Claim

Governed execution transforms intent into explicit contracts before any tool
or runtime action occurs.

This remains an architectural argument. The live AI/research chapter records
the claim at `argument` support. The source notes, schemas, fixtures,
synthetic plan-execution harness, and finite Lean predicates support record
discipline for intent, command, planning, job, artifact, and approval
boundaries. They do not prove a deployed runtime, replayed vertical slice,
artifact satisfaction, parser quality, tool-effect enforcement, approval
service behavior, benchmark performance, or end-to-end execution correctness.

The claim is therefore not that the repo already executes intent. The claim is
that governed execution should not begin until intent has been converted into
a contract whose fields can be inspected, lowered, blocked, verified, and
revisited.

## Mechanism

The intent-to-execution layer turns a request into a trace.

The trace begins with an intent contract. It records the request summary,
desired outcome, allowed means, forbidden means, authority ceiling, source
boundaries, acceptance criteria, evidence requirements, escalation conditions,
stop conditions, open ambiguities, bounded defaults, re-contract triggers, and
non-claims.

The command contract narrows that intent into operational language. The plan
graph decomposes the command. Typed jobs inherit the relevant constraints.
Authority transitions say which side effects are allowed, denied, expired, or
pending approval. Runtime adapters may act only when the authority gate
allows them. Artifacts point back to the parent intent. Verification records
decide whether local outputs satisfy the relevant criteria. Feedback updates
claims, regressions, tools, and residuals.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  Intent["Human intent"] --> IC["Intent contract"]
  IC --> CC["Command contract"]
  CC --> Plan["Plan graph"]
  Plan --> Jobs["Typed jobs"]
  Jobs --> Gate{"Authority and approval gate"}
  Gate -- "allowed" --> Runtime["Runtime adapter or tool"]
  Gate -- "missing or denied" --> Stop["Stop, residual, or re-contract"]
  Runtime --> Artifact["Artifact"]
  Artifact --> Verify["Verification record"]
  Verify --> Feedback["Feedback and evidence ledger"]
  Stop --> Feedback
```

The important feature is not motion. It is controlled handoff. Each layer
receives a bounded object rather than an ambient desire. Each layer either
produces the next artifact or records why it cannot. If a planner discovers
that the objective is underspecified, the tool exceeds authority, the context
is inadequate, the verifier cannot check the output, or the cost exceeds the
budget, the correct move is re-contract, escalation, deferral, or refusal.

Helpful overrun becomes a failure mode. The system may predict that the human
would appreciate extra work, but prediction is not authority. Extra scope has
to become a new contract request.

## Interfaces

Intent enters execution through the Intent-to-Execution Trace.

Minimum fields span the parent intent contract and the trace:

- `trace_id`
- `intent_contract`
- `intake_state`
- `command_contract`
- `plan_graph`
- `typed_jobs`
- `authority_transitions`
- `handoff_receipts`
- `dispatch_receipts`
- `artifacts`
- `verification_records`
- `delivery_or_deployment`
- `feedback_refs`
- `recontract_events`
- `stop_or_fault_state`
- `residuals`
- `bounded_defaults`
- `recontract_triggers`
- `non_claims`

Intent contracts feed planning. Command contracts bind procedure and output
requirements. Plan graphs organize dependency order. Typed jobs carry
executable units. Authority transitions control side effects. Runtime adapters
act only inside approved authority. Verification records compare artifacts
against acceptance criteria. Evidence ledgers record satisfaction, failure,
repair, and residuals.

Authority deltas are part of the interface. Intent may authorize a goal.
Command contracts narrow the means. Plans allocate tasks. Jobs request tools.
Runtime adapters request effects. Each step can preserve, narrow, expire,
deny, or require approval. It cannot silently widen.

## Invariants

Contract constraints survive compilation.

Side effects require explicit execution authority.

Artifacts remain linked to source intent.

Acceptance remains separate from production. Producing an artifact, running a
tool, or finishing a subtask is not the same as satisfying the parent intent.
The trace must keep local completion separate from contract satisfaction.

Residual obligations survive partial success. A useful artifact can still
leave unanswered questions, failed checks, missing approvals, or deferred
work. Those residuals must travel forward rather than being erased by the
fact that something was delivered.

Contract closure requires an explicit acceptance state, not merely the absence
of an error.

## Failure modes

Response mistaken for completed work is the first failure. The system writes
something that sounds finished while leaving tests, evidence, approvals,
source boundaries, or artifact identity outside the record.

Artifact identity loss breaks audit and repair. Later systems cannot inspect,
reproduce, roll back, or improve work if they cannot connect the artifact to
the intent, command, job, verifier, feedback, and residuals that produced it.

Approval bypass turns planning into execution authority. A plan can describe a
tool path, but description is not permission to run the tool.

Helpful overrun happens when the system expands scope because the expansion
seems useful. Governed execution treats that as a re-contract event, not a
success.

Stale acceptance happens when an old approval is reused after the artifact,
tool, authority, cost, or risk tier changed.

Trace laundering happens when a shaped record is treated as execution
evidence. A valid schema instance is not a run. A synthetic fixture is not a
deployed runtime. A Lean predicate over finite records is not artifact
satisfaction.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The smallest honest implementation is an `intent_contract` schema, a
`command_contract` schema, an `intent_execution_trace` schema, and a two-path
public-safe trace fixture.

One path should lower an intent into a command, plan, typed job, authority
transition, artifact reference, verification record, feedback record, and
accepted residual state. The second path should stop because authority,
context, approval, verifier, or budget is missing. The stop path is as
important as the success path because governed execution needs visible
non-execution states.

The current repository already has the schema surfaces, protocol fixture
validation, the synthetic plan-execution contract harness, and finite Lean
predicates for parent-constraint preservation and missing-approval blocking.
Those checks are useful controls. They validate public record shape and
synthetic cross-record consistency. They do not show that a real request was
parsed, planned, authorized, executed, verified, accepted, or replayed.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature endpoint is a contract operating spine for the whole stack.

Every serious request would enter as a durable intake object. It would receive
an explicit authority envelope, lower through command, plan, job, runtime,
artifact, verification, delivery, feedback, and residual records, and remain
inspectable after the work is done. A human, reviewer, runtime, or later AI
agent could ask what was requested, what was authorized, which defaults were
used, which re-contract events occurred, which jobs ran, which side effects
were approved, which artifacts were produced, which validators accepted them,
and which residuals remain.

This would make stopping normal. Missing context, missing approval, missing
verifier, budget exhaustion, violated constraints, or forbidden means would
not be conversational failures. They would be governed stop states.

The final product surface would look less like a chat transcript and more like
a work ledger with contract intelligence: intake contracts, lowering receipts,
authority transitions, typed jobs, runtime receipts, artifact identities,
verification records, feedback links, and residual ledgers. Later optimization
could improve each layer only by preserving the contract trail.

That endpoint remains a target architecture. The current support state stays
at `argument` until replayed execution traces, acceptance receipts, approval
records, artifact checks, and review logs show that useful results were also
contract-linked, authority-preserving, and accepted under their stated
criteria.

## Summary

Intent-to-Execution Contracts make the first operational handoff in Part II.

They turn human purpose into a traceable chain of intent, command, plan, job,
authority, artifact, verification, feedback, and residuals. The point is not
paperwork. The point is continuity: requirements, authority, artifacts, and
evidence have to survive the trip from purpose to action.

A downstream layer should never have to guess whether it is allowed to act. If
it has to guess, the trace should stop, ask, narrow, or re-contract.

## Handoff

Intent contracts preserve purpose, authority, and residuals across the first
translation from human request to governed work. That translation has to
become compact enough for machines to validate without turning language into
hidden permission. Command Contracts and Semantic Interfaces follows by
supplying the operational language: fields, defaults, failure behavior, and
verification duties that planners can consume without guessing what the human
meant.
